MASTER CALENDAR JURY

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Jun-09-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT JEWISH COMMUNITY CENTER OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT

No appearances. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Off calendar. Notice of settlement of entire action filed on 5/19/26. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/CVA)

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-26-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows

DEFENDANT JEWISH COMMUNITY CENTER OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT.

Defendant Jewish Community Center's motion to strike portions of plaintiff Linda Camarena's First Amended Complaint is denied. A motion to strike lies to remove irrelevant, false, or improper matter, or parts of a pleading not in conformity with law, court rules, or court orders. (Code Civ. Proc. 435.) Camarena's pleading about JCC's alleged violation of various laws in operating its facilities is not irrelevant, false, or improper. JCC is correct that negligence per se is not a separate cause of action, but there is nothing improper about alleging a violation of law in a complaint for negligence.

Proposed order is submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-26-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT JEWISH COMMUNITY CENTER OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Defendant Jewish Community Center's demurrer to plaintiff Linda Camarena's first amended complaint is sustained with leave to amend as to the first cause of action, overruled as to the third cause of action, and sustained without leave to amend as to the fourth cause of action.

[SEE WORD MINUTES FOR COMPLETE RULING]

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)

MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-31-2025 Continued To Jan-26-26 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 301

PLAINTIFF LINDA CAMERENA MOTION FOR LEAVE TO TO FILE A FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT; DECLARATION OF LIGIA M. PARMENTER - EXHIBIT "A" PROPOSED FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT (TENATIVE RULING PART 1, SEE PART 2)
Off Calendar Sep-10-2025 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR LEAVE TO To File A First Amended Complaint; Declaration Of Ligia M. Parmenter  Exhibit A Proposed First Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Sep-10-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF LINDA CAMERENA's MOTION FOR LEAVE TO To File A First Amended Complaint.

No appearances made in this matter. The court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Linda Camarena's motion for leave to file a first amended complaint is granted.

This court should grant leave for a party to amend a pleading "in furtherance of justice." (Code Civ. Proc., section 473, subd. (a)(1).) "That trial courts are to liberally permit such amendments, at any stage of the proceeding, has been established policy in this state since 1901." (Hirsa v. Superior Court (1981) 118 Cal.App.3d 486, 488-89.) Although amendments are liberally granted, leave to amend is properly denied when the proposed amendment fails to state a valid cause of action and thus would be futile, or where the plaintiff has delayed in seeking the amendment and the delay has prejudiced the defendant. (Atkinson v. Elk Corp. (2003) 109 Cal.App.4th 739.)

Here, the proposed amendment would not be futile. While a proposed amendment must state a valid cause of action, "that rule would find its most appropriate application in cases in which the insufficiency of the proposed amendment is established by controlling precedent and where the insufficiency could not be cured by further appropriate amendment." (California Casualty General Insurance Co. v. Superior Court (1985) 173 Cal.App.3d 274, 280-81.) There is not indication here that the proposed causes of action based on negligence per se or California Civil Code section 1812.82 would be futile. Therefore, "the preferable practice would be to permit the amendment and allow the parties to test its legal sufficiency by demurrer, motion for judgment on the pleadings or other appropriate proceedings." (Ibid.)

Additionally, although Camarena has waited almost two years to seek leave to amend the complaint, that delay has not prejudiced the Jewish Community Center. In Magpali v. Farmers Group, Inc. (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 471, the court denied a motion to amend filed after a similar delay, but in that case the motion came on the eve of trial. Here, trial is not set for until October 5, 2026, ample time to prepare for trial on the new claims.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
